name: Late-life rapamycin extended lifespan in genetically heterogeneous mice
summary: The Harrison 2009 ITP paper is the core mammalian lifespan anchor for Rapalogs: late-life dietary rapamycin extended mouse lifespan across sites, but remains animal evidence rather than proof of human longevity benefit.
statement: Dietary rapamycin begun at 600 days of age extended median and maximal lifespan in both male and female genetically heterogeneous mice, with age at 90% mortality increased by 14% in females and 9% in males.
population_or_model: Genetically heterogeneous mice treated at three independent ITP sites
time_horizon: Late-life treatment beginning at 600 days with lifespan follow-up
quantitative_note: The abstract reports age-at-90%-mortality increases of 14% in females and 9% in males, plus increased survival in a separate 270-day-start cohort.
caveats: This is animal lifespan evidence, not human longevity evidence.
Mouse lifespan benefit does not settle human dose, schedule, safety, or endpoint relevance.
The authors noted lifespan could reflect postponed cancer, retarded aging mechanisms, or both.
